claim: In March 2000, the Vienna-based art collective Gelitin (also spelled 'Gelatin') installed an unauthorized wooden balcony on the 91st floor of WTC 1 (North Tower). The collective gained access through a Lower Manhattan Cultural Council artist-residency program, removed the putty around a window pane and detached the glass, and four members in costume briefly stepped onto the balcony while a helicopter was commissioned to document the action. The project, titled 'The B-Thing,' was published as a book in 2001. The LMCC issued a memo denying that any such installation had occurred.

sources.label: Rolling Stone — “The Artwork That Spawned 9/11 Conspiracy Theories and Mystery”
Spike Art Magazine — “One Work: Gelitin's 'The B-Thing'”
sources.quote: One by one, wearing angel wings and brightly colored T-shirts, each of the four Gelitin boys made their way onto the balcony.

sources.label: Gelitin (Gelatin), The B-Thing (Walther König, 2001) — primary publication by the artists
Haley Press / @strategichaley, Instagram reel DVOtv6QCTFf (Feb 26, 2026) — surfacing source

notes: The reel's lines about 'we'd make you a little art project' and 'group photo on the last day' map onto the Gelitin B-Thing. The art project's existence is documented in the artists' own book and ongoing art-press coverage; the unauthorized access via LMCC residency is also documented. URLs not directly fetched in this pass. Note: 91st floor of the North Tower was the boundary above which no occupants survived.
